does: NeuroArmor introduces a white-box runtime defense mechanism for large language models that generates prompt-specific safe variants to serve as local safety references. The method constructs K safe variants for each input prompt, then monitors the model's hidden states to detect when a prompt deviates from this safe reference distribution. Upon detection, the system routes the prompt to either a refusal branch for malicious inputs or a helpful recovery branch for borderline benign requests that need correction. Evaluated on Llama-3-8B-Instruct, NeuroArmor substantially reduces jailbreak attack success rates from 41.56% to 1.57% while simultaneously decreasing false positive rates on benign prompts, achieving improved balance between safety and utility compared to existing defenses.
connects: This work extends recent research on runtime defenses for LLMs that operate in hidden-state space, building on prior work exploring representation-based safety mechanisms. It responds to limitations in existing defenses that apply uniform blocking strategies regardless of prompt type, which leads to either missing attacks or over-blocking benign content. NeuroArmor connects to research on safe-variant generation and in-context safety steering, as well as detection-based defense paradigms that identify when intervention is necessary. The method also relates to work on understanding jailbreak mechanisms including role-play, translation, encoding attacks, and adversarial suffix techniques, integrating defenses against this diverse threat landscape.
enables: This approach opens directions for more sophisticated adaptive defense mechanisms that tailor interventions to specific prompt characteristics. Future work could explore learning better safe-variant generation strategies, scaling this method to larger models, and combining it with other defense layers. The selective re-anchoring mechanism enables research into fine-grained safety controls that distinguish between harmful, borderline, and benign requests, potentially improving user experience while maintaining security. The white-box nature of the defense creates opportunities to understand model vulnerabilities deeper and develop stronger invariant-based safety guarantees, supporting development of inherently more robust language models.
